title: Beyond the Unruh vacuum: multi-time correlations in black hole collapse and evaporation
authors: Konstantinos Xenos, Charis Anastopoulos, Andreas F. Terzis
abstract: The black hole information paradox originates from the thermal character of Hawking radiation, which appears to erase information about the collapsing matter. However, thermality constrains only observables defined at a single time and leaves the structure of temporal quantum correlations largely unexplored. Here we show that multi-time quantum-field correlations provide a concrete mechanism for the survival of pre-collapse information in black hole evaporation. Using a two-dimensional model of gravitational collapse and evaporation, we demonstrate that late-time multi-time correlations are not fully reproduced by the Unruh vacuum. In particular, they contain a contribution that depends explicitly on parameters characterizing the pre-collapse state, despite the thermal character of the asymptotic radiation. Our results identify measurable multi-time correlations as carriers of information in Hawking radiation and suggest that formulations of the black hole information paradox based solely on single-time observables are incomplete.
summary: The paper argues that the black hole information paradox is based on an incomplete picture derived only from single-time measurements of Hawking radiation. By analyzing higher-order, multi-time quantum correlations, the authors demonstrate a 'memory term' that explicitly carries information about the matter that collapsed, suggesting these temporal correlations are key to resolving the paradox.
main_problem: The black hole information paradox arises because Hawking radiation appears thermal, suggesting information loss. The authors investigate if pre-collapse information can survive in measurable correlations beyond single-time observables.
main_result: Multi-time quantum-field correlations (specifically the memory term $C^{(2)}_{	ext{mem}}$) are shown to depend explicitly on parameters characterizing the initial pre-collapse state, indicating that information is encoded in higher-order temporal correlations.
method: The analysis uses multi-time correlation functions, such as the second-order coherence function ($g^{(2)}$), calculated using advanced techniques like the Schwinger–Keldysh formalism and complex contour integration.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum field ($\hat{\phi}$) undergoing gravitational collapse and evaporation in a two-dimensional model. The analysis contrasts measurements constrained by single-time observables (like those from the Hawking-Wald theorem) with joint probability distributions for multiple detection events.
key_observables: Second-order coherence function $g^{(2)}(t_1, E_1; t_2, E_2)$, decomposed into Unruh vacuum ($C^{(2)}_U$), memory ($C^{(2)}_{	ext{mem}}$), and correlation ($C^{(2)}_{	ext{corr}}$) terms.
important_parameters: Pre-collapse state parameters (e.g., $D_0$), energy differences ($\Delta E$), and time separations ($\Delta t$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that multi-time measurements are possible and that the thermal approximation based solely on the reduced density matrix is incomplete.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate complex contours used for evaluating correlation integrals, showing the decomposition of coherence functions into Unruh components and non-Unruh memory/correlation parts.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing that single-time measurements yield thermal results (like the Unruh vacuum), then introducing multi-time correlations via $g^{(2)}$, and finally demonstrating mathematically that specific terms in this correlation function retain dependence on initial conditions, violating pure thermality.
why_interesting: This work extends concepts from open quantum systems theory to extreme astrophysical environments, showing how non-Markovian effects (captured by multi-time correlations) can preserve information otherwise lost under single-time approximations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Penrose diagram of an evaporating black hole. Two measurement events a and b lie on distinct late-time Cauchy surfaces KAι0 and KBι0 and are spacelike separated, implying vanishing correlations in a local description. When projected onto future null infinity I +, the corresponding points a′ and b′ appear causally connected. The figure illustrates that projection to null infinity does not preserve the full spacetime relation between detection events, motivating a real-time treatment of correlations.

FIG. 2. The amplitudes of the three contributions C(2) U , C(2) corr and C(2) mem to the second-order coherence function for Hawking radiation are plotted as a function of E2/TH for fixed E1 = TH, where TH = 1/8πM is the Hawking temperature.
FIG. 3. The contour used for the integration of I(∆˜ U). We consider the lower half plane where we avoid the branch cuts created by the branch points zk = i(8πM)k.
FIG. 4. Graphical representation of the integral I(∆v)(∆v) A , corresponding to a combination of the pairs (v1, v2) and (v′ 1, v′ 2).
FIG. 5. The Unruh-vacuum terms of the A-channel (left) and of the B-channel (right).
